canada_mac.title: Facts & Figures – The State of Canada's Mining Industry
canada_mac.level_1: The Mining Association of Canada (MAC) has released its annual Facts & Figures report for 2026, summarizing the economic health, employment metrics, and critical competitiveness challenges facing the nation's minerals sector. In 2024, the mining industry directly contributed $111 billion to Canada's Gross Domestic Product (GDP), representing 3.6% of the national economy. When oil and gas extraction are added, the broader natural resources extraction sector contributes over 5.2% of total national GDP. In terms of employment, the mining and mineral processing sector directly employs 438,000 workers across the country, supporting an additional 272,000 indirect jobs in supply chain services, engineering, and equipment manufacturing. Notably, the mining industry remains one of the largest private-sector employers of Indigenous Peoples in Canada. According to 2024 census updates, the sector employs 22,600 Indigenous workers, primarily in remote and northern communities, accounting for over 7.5% of the total mining workforce. However, the report highlights that the sector has experienced stagnant real growth over the past two decades. The inflation-adjusted GDP of the Canadian mining sector in 2024 remained at levels virtually identical to those of 2007, raising critical concerns about regulatory and infrastructure bottlenecks that are stalling new project development.
canada_mac.level_2: The Mining Association of Canada (MAC) has released its annual Facts & Figures report for 2026, summarizing the economic health, employment metrics, and critical competitiveness challenges facing the nation's minerals sector. In 2024, the mining industry directly contributed $111 billion to Canada's Gross Domestic Product (GDP), representing 3.6% of the national economy. When oil and gas extraction are added, the broader natural resources extraction sector contributes over 5.2% of total national GDP. In terms of employment, the mining and mineral processing sector directly employs 438,000 workers across the country, supporting an additional 272,000 indirect jobs in supply chain services, engineering, and equipment manufacturing. Notably, the mining industry remains one of the largest private-sector employers of Indigenous Peoples in Canada. According to 2024 census updates, the sector employs 22,600 Indigenous workers, primarily in remote and northern communities, accounting for over 7.5% of the total mining workforce. However, the report highlights that the sector has experienced stagnant real growth over the past two decades. The inflation-adjusted GDP of the Canadian mining sector in 2024 remained at levels virtually identical to those of 2007, raising critical concerns about regulatory and infrastructure bottlenecks that are stalling new project development. Additionally, mining exports reached a record $152 billion in 2024, representing 21% of all Canadian merchandise exports. The United States remains Canada's largest minerals trading partner, receiving 51% of all export shipments, followed by the European Union (18%) and China (12%). The report expresses deep concern over output trends for critical transition minerals: since 2015, Canadian production of copper has declined by 23%, nickel production has plummeted by 44%, and iron ore production has dropped by 6%. This production decline is attributed to depleted reserves, declining ore grades, and a lack of greenfield exploration success, which threatens Canada's ability to supply the essential raw materials required for the global clean energy transition.
canada_mac.level_3: The Mining Association of Canada (MAC) has released its annual Facts & Figures report for 2026, summarizing the economic health, employment metrics, and critical competitiveness challenges facing the nation's minerals sector. In 2024, the mining industry directly contributed $111 billion to Canada's Gross Domestic Product (GDP), representing 3.6% of the national economy. When oil and gas extraction are added, the broader natural resources extraction sector contributes over 5.2% of total national GDP. In terms of employment, the mining and mineral processing sector directly employs 438,000 workers across the country, supporting an additional 272,000 indirect jobs in supply chain services, engineering, and equipment manufacturing. Notably, the mining industry remains one of the largest private-sector employers of Indigenous Peoples in Canada. According to 2024 census updates, the sector employs 22,600 Indigenous workers, primarily in remote and northern communities, accounting for over 7.5% of the total mining workforce. However, the report highlights that the sector has experienced stagnant real growth over the past two decades. The inflation-adjusted GDP of the Canadian mining sector in 2024 remained at levels virtually identical to those of 2007, raising critical concerns about regulatory and infrastructure bottlenecks that are stalling new project development. Additionally, mining exports reached a record $152 billion in 2024, representing 21% of all Canadian merchandise exports. The United States remains Canada's largest minerals trading partner, receiving 51% of all export shipments, followed by the European Union (18%) and China (12%). The report expresses deep concern over output trends for critical transition minerals: since 2015, Canadian production of copper has declined by 23%, nickel production has plummeted by 44%, and iron ore production has dropped by 6%. This production decline is attributed to depleted reserves, declining ore grades, and a lack of greenfield exploration success, which threatens Canada's ability to supply the essential raw materials required for the global clean energy transition. The report provides a granular look at provincial mining output. Ontario remains the largest producer with a total mineral production value of $13.5 billion, followed by Quebec at $12.8 billion and British Columbia at $11.2 billion. The report calls for urgent policy remediation in three key areas. First, it criticizes the regulatory delays associated with the federal Impact Assessment Act (IAA), pointing out that it takes an average of 12 to 15 years to bring a new critical mineral mine from discovery to production in Canada, compared to 5 to 7 years in competitive jurisdictions like Australia. Second, the report highlights massive infrastructure deficits in northern regions, particularly the lack of all-weather roads and grid connectivity in the Ring of Fire region of northern Ontario, which holds multi-billion dollar deposits of nickel, copper, and chromite. Third, MAC calls for the full implementation of the Federal Critical Minerals Strategy, urging the government to expand and accelerate the Clean Technology Manufacturing Tax Credit (30% tax credit for extraction, processing, and recycling of critical minerals) and to provide targeted capital grants for remote, off-grid mines attempting to transition from diesel generation to renewable microgrids, estimating that remote mines face an average carbon tax liability of $4.2 million annually if they cannot displace diesel fuel.

australia_mca.title: Minerals Council of Australia Annual Report 2025
australia_mca.level_1: The Minerals Council of Australia (MCA) has published its Annual Report, outlining the performance, advocacy, and economic impact of Australia's minerals sector. The report highlights the mining sector's role as Australia's largest taxpayer. In the 2023-24 financial year, the minerals sector contributed a record $59.4 billion in company taxes and royalties to federal, state, and territory governments. This represents a critical source of funding for public services, including healthcare, education, and defense. Over the past decade (2014-15 to 2023-24), the Australian minerals sector has contributed a cumulative total of $394.6 billion in company taxes and royalties, underscoring the industry's sustained economic significance. The MCA, which represents more than 90 full member companies and 40 associate members accounting for over 85% of Australia's minerals production, emphasizes the need for competitive fiscal policies and regulatory stability to maintain this level of national contribution.
australia_mca.level_2: The Minerals Council of Australia (MCA) has published its Annual Report, outlining the performance, advocacy, and economic impact of Australia's minerals sector. The report highlights the mining sector's role as Australia's largest taxpayer. In the 2023-24 financial year, the minerals sector contributed a record $59.4 billion in company taxes and royalties to federal, state, and territory governments. This represents a critical source of funding for public services, including healthcare, education, and defense. Over the past decade (2014-15 to 2023-24), the Australian minerals sector has contributed a cumulative total of $394.6 billion in company taxes and royalties, underscoring the industry's sustained economic significance. The MCA, which represents more than 90 full member companies and 40 associate members accounting for over 85% of Australia's minerals production, emphasizes the need for competitive fiscal policies and regulatory stability to maintain this level of national contribution. The report also highlights export and employment statistics. Over the last decade, Australia's resources sector generated a total of $2.9 trillion in export revenues. The industry directly employed 303,300 workers in the 2023-24 financial year, with approximately 87% of these positions being permanent, full-time roles. The broader Minerals Equipment, Technology and Services (METS) sector supports an additional 1.1 million jobs nationwide, proving that the mining value chain has a massive multiplier effect across the Australian economy. In terms of regional performance, the MCA highlights that the minerals sector contributed $569.1 million in direct spending in Victoria and supported a growing mineral resources boom in the Northern Territory, boosting local employment and infrastructure development.
australia_mca.level_3: The Minerals Council of Australia (MCA) has published its Annual Report, outlining the performance, advocacy, and economic impact of Australia's minerals sector. The report highlights the mining sector's role as Australia's largest taxpayer. In the 2023-24 financial year, the minerals sector contributed a record $59.4 billion in company taxes and royalties to federal, state, and territory governments. This represents a critical source of funding for public services, including healthcare, education, and defense. Over the past decade (2014-15 to 2023-24), the Australian minerals sector has contributed a cumulative total of $394.6 billion in company taxes and royalties, underscoring the industry's sustained economic significance. The MCA, which represents more than 90 full member companies and 40 associate members accounting for over 85% of Australia's minerals production, emphasizes the need for competitive fiscal policies and regulatory stability to maintain this level of national contribution. The report also highlights export and employment statistics. Over the last decade, Australia's resources sector generated a total of $2.9 trillion in export revenues. The industry directly employed 303,300 workers in the 2023-24 financial year, with approximately 87% of these positions being permanent, full-time roles. The broader Minerals Equipment, Technology and Services (METS) sector supports an additional 1.1 million jobs nationwide, proving that the mining value chain has a massive multiplier effect across the Australian economy. In terms of regional performance, the MCA highlights that the minerals sector contributed $569.1 million in direct spending in Victoria and supported a growing mineral resources boom in the Northern Territory, boosting local employment and infrastructure development. The report details the national rollout of the Towards Sustainable Mining (TSM) Australia framework, which provides an internationally recognized standard for site-level sustainability, focusing on community engagement, tailings management, and water stewardship. In its advocacy section, the MCA outlines key policy submissions to the federal government. First, it warns against proposed changes to multi-employer bargaining and labor hire rules, which it estimates could cost the mining sector $1.2 billion annually in lost productivity and administrative costs. Second, it calls for a streamlined, single-track environmental assessment process, supporting the government's $105.9 million budget allocation to expedite assessments under the Environment Protection and Biodiversity Conservation (EPBC) Act using AI technologies. Finally, the report highlights the MCA's critical minerals strategy, 'Minerals-plus', urging the government to expand the Critical Minerals Facility to $4 billion to provide low-cost debt for downstream processing plants, such as nickel sulphate and lithium hydroxide refineries, ensuring Australia captures more value from the global green energy supply chain.

china_mnr.title: China Mineral Resources 2024
china_mnr.source: Ministry of Natural Resources, People's Republic of China
china_mnr.level_1: The Ministry of Natural Resources (MNR) of the People's Republic of China has published the China Mineral Resources 2024 report. This official publication reviews the state of China's domestic mineral exploration, reserves, and production. In 2023, China's geological exploration investment reached 115 billion RMB ($16.4 billion), demonstrating a significant state-driven effort to secure resource self-sufficiency. Exploration efforts resulted in the discovery of 124 new mineral deposits. The report details substantial increases in the domestic reserves of critical minerals: lithium reserves grew by 24%, cobalt reserves increased by 11%, and nickel reserves increased by 8%. The Ministry emphasizes the importance of strengthening domestic exploration ('the new round of strategic mining breakthrough actions') to mitigate reliance on foreign imports for transition metals.
china_mnr.level_2: The Ministry of Natural Resources (MNR) of the People's Republic of China has published the China Mineral Resources 2024 report. This official publication reviews the state of China's domestic mineral exploration, reserves, and production. In 2023, China's geological exploration investment reached 115 billion RMB ($16.4 billion), demonstrating a significant state-driven effort to secure resource self-sufficiency. Exploration efforts resulted in the discovery of 124 new mineral deposits. The report details substantial increases in the domestic reserves of critical minerals: lithium reserves grew by 24%, cobalt reserves increased by 11%, and nickel reserves increased by 8%. The Ministry emphasizes the importance of strengthening domestic exploration ('the new round of strategic mining breakthrough actions') to mitigate reliance on foreign imports for transition metals. China maintained its global dominance in the refining and production of copper, steel, and aluminum, accounting for over 54% of global refined steel output in 2023. The report outlines the national 'Green Mining' standards, published by the China Mining Association (CMA), which mandate that new mining operations must integrate ecological restoration and energy-saving technologies. Over 1,000 mines nationwide have been officially certified under the National Green Mine Registry. The government has also reformed its mineral rights system, transitioning to a competitive public bidding and auction model to optimize resource allocation and encourage private exploration investment.
china_mnr.level_3: The Ministry of Natural Resources (MNR) of the People's Republic of China has published the China Mineral Resources 2024 report. This official publication reviews the state of China's domestic mineral exploration, reserves, and production. In 2023, China's geological exploration investment reached 115 billion RMB ($16.4 billion), demonstrating a significant state-driven effort to secure resource self-sufficiency. Exploration efforts resulted in the discovery of 124 new mineral deposits. The report details substantial increases in the domestic reserves of critical minerals: lithium reserves grew by 24%, cobalt reserves increased by 11%, and nickel reserves increased by 8%. The Ministry emphasizes the importance of strengthening domestic exploration ('the new round of strategic mining breakthrough actions') to mitigate reliance on foreign imports for transition metals. China maintained its global dominance in the refining and production of copper, steel, and aluminum, accounting for over 54% of global refined steel output in 2023. The report outlines the national 'Green Mining' standards, published by the China Mining Association (CMA), which mandate that new mining operations must integrate ecological restoration and energy-saving technologies. Over 1,000 mines nationwide have been officially certified under the National Green Mine Registry. The government has also reformed its mineral rights system, transitioning to a competitive public bidding and auction model to optimize resource allocation and encourage private exploration investment. The report dedicates a significant section to international cooperation under the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI). Chinese mining firms invested $8.2 billion in overseas mining projects in 2023, primarily in Southeast Asia, Africa, and South America, focusing on copper, cobalt, and lithium assets. Despite domestic reserve growth, China's import dependency for iron ore remained high at 75%, and copper concentrate import dependency reached 78%. To address this, the government is promoting the development of deep-earth exploration technologies (targeting deposits below 1,000 meters) and intelligent, fully automated mines using 5G and autonomous haulage systems. In terms of environmental remediation, the report notes that the government allocated 4.5 billion RMB in 2023 for the ecological restoration of abandoned mines, reclaiming over 32,000 hectares of land for agricultural and forestry use.

global_icmm.title: ICMM Annual Review 2024
global_icmm.source: International Council on Mining and Metals
global_icmm.level_1: The International Council on Mining and Metals (ICMM) has released its Annual Review, highlighting the global mining industry's progress toward environmental, social, and governance (ESG) standards. A central highlight of the report is the formal adoption of the Nature Position Statement and its associated 5-Point Plan, which commits ICMM's 25 member companies—representing about one-third of the global mining industry—to contributing to a 'nature positive' future aligned with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. The strategy requires members to halt biodiversity loss at their operating sites, conserve pristine natural habitats, and collaborate across their value chains to ensure sustainable resource sourcing.
global_icmm.level_2: The International Council on Mining and Metals (ICMM) has released its Annual Review, highlighting the global mining industry's progress toward environmental, social, and governance (ESG) standards. A central highlight of the report is the formal adoption of the Nature Position Statement and its associated 5-Point Plan, which commits ICMM's 25 member companies—representing about one-third of the global mining industry—to contributing to a 'nature positive' future aligned with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. The strategy requires members to halt biodiversity loss at their operating sites, conserve pristine natural habitats, and collaborate across their value chains to ensure sustainable resource sourcing. The review outlines the details of the 5-Point Plan: 1) Protecting and conserving pristine areas, 2) Halting biodiversity loss at operations, 3) Collaborating across value chains, 4) Restoring and enhancing landscapes, and 5) Catalyzing wider ecological change. Additionally, the report compiles safety and performance data across member companies. In 2023, member operations recorded a total of 31 occupational fatalities, a decrease from 33 in 2022. The report emphasizes the council's commitment to 'Target Zero' fatalities through the deployment of critical control management systems, particularly targeting vehicle interactions and geotechnical stability.
global_icmm.level_3: The International Council on Mining and Metals (ICMM) has released its Annual Review, highlighting the global mining industry's progress toward environmental, social, and governance (ESG) standards. A central highlight of the report is the formal adoption of the Nature Position Statement and its associated 5-Point Plan, which commits ICMM's 25 member companies—representing about one-third of the global mining industry—to contributing to a 'nature positive' future aligned with the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. The strategy requires members to halt biodiversity loss at their operating sites, conserve pristine natural habitats, and collaborate across their value chains to ensure sustainable resource sourcing. The review outlines the details of the 5-Point Plan: 1) Protecting and conserving pristine areas, 2) Halting biodiversity loss at operations, 3) Collaborating across value chains, 4) Restoring and enhancing landscapes, and 5) Catalyzing wider ecological change. Additionally, the report compiles safety and performance data across member companies. In 2023, member operations recorded a total of 31 occupational fatalities, a decrease from 33 in 2022. The report emphasizes the council's commitment to 'Target Zero' fatalities through the deployment of critical control management systems, particularly targeting vehicle interactions and geotechnical stability. The review provides detailed updates on the implementation of the Global Industry Standard on Tailings Management (GISTM), noting that 85% of members' high-consequence tailings facilities have now achieved full compliance with the standard's rigorous engineering and governance criteria. ICMM is also working in partnership with the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD), leading pilot programs across 12 member sites to develop sector-specific nature reporting guidelines. On water stewardship, the report notes that 92% of member sites have implemented local water management plans that account for cumulative watershed impacts. Finally, the report highlights the circular economy, outlining a collective commitment to double the use of recycled secondary metals in members' processing plants by 2035, reducing the primary carbon and environmental footprint of the minerals supply chain.
